---
title: Basic Usage
description: createEnv options, groups, sources, validation behavior, client prefix, and hot reload.
icon: Play
---

## createEnv

```ts
import { createEnv } from "@justwant/env";
import { z } from "zod";

const env = createEnv({
  vars: {
    PORT: z.string().transform(Number),
    DATABASE_URL: z.string().url(),
    NODE_ENV: z.enum(["development", "production", "test"]).default("development"),
  },
});

env.PORT         // number
env.DATABASE_URL // string
env.NODE_ENV     // "development" | "production" | "test"
```

## Validation errors

By default, missing or invalid variables cause `createEnv` to throw an `EnvironmentError`:

```
EnvironmentError: 2 invalid environment variable(s)

  DATABASE_URL   → Invalid url
  SECRET_KEY   → Required
```

To change the error behavior:

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  vars: { ... },
  validation: {
    onError: "warn",   // "throw" (default) | "warn" | "silent" | false
  },
});
```

To skip validation entirely (e.g. in tests):

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  vars: { ... },
  validation: { skip: true },
});
```

## Groups

Groups let you namespace a set of variables under a common prefix. Environment variables are read as `GROUP_KEY` (uppercase, with `_` separator by default) and accessed as `env.group.key`.

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  groups: {
    db: {
      host: z.string(),
      port: z.string().transform(Number),
      name: z.string(),
    },
    redis: {
      url: z.string().url(),
    },
  },
});

env.db.host   // string — reads DB_HOST
env.db.port   // number — reads DB_PORT
env.redis.url // string — reads REDIS_URL
```

```bash title=".env"
DB_HOST=localhost
DB_PORT=5432
DB_NAME=myapp
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379
```

## Sources

By default, `createEnv` reads `process.env` and `.env` files. You can customize this:

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  vars: { ... },
  sources: {
    // Override the files to load
    files: [".env", ".env.local"],
    // Use process.env (default: true)
    processEnv: true,
    // Inject inline values (useful in tests)
    inline: { PORT: "3001" },
  },
});
```

## Variable expansion

When `expand: true` (default), values can reference other variables:

```bash title=".env"
BASE_URL=https://api.example.com
USERS_URL=${BASE_URL}/users
```

To disable expansion:

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  vars: { ... },
  expand: false,
});
```

## Client prefix

Mark variables that are safe to include in client-side bundles (e.g. Next.js `NEXT_PUBLIC_`):

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  vars: {
    ANALYTICS_ID: z.string(),
    APP_NAME: z.string(),
  },
  clientPrefix: "NEXT_PUBLIC_",
});
```

With this prefix, `createEnv` reads `NEXT_PUBLIC_ANALYTICS_ID` from the environment and exposes it as `env.ANALYTICS_ID`.

## defineEnv (shared definitions)

Use `defineEnv` to define a reusable env block that can be included in multiple `createEnv` calls — useful when sharing between server and client entry points.

```ts title="lib/client-env.ts"
import { defineEnv } from "@justwant/env";
import { z } from "zod";

export const clientEnv = defineEnv({
  vars: {
    APP_NAME: z.string(),
    ANALYTICS_ID: z.string(),
  },
  clientPrefix: "NEXT_PUBLIC_",
});
```

```ts title="lib/server-env.ts"
import { createEnv } from "@justwant/env";
import { z } from "zod";
import { clientEnv } from "./client-env";

export const env = createEnv({
  include: [clientEnv],
  vars: {
    DATABASE_URL: z.string().url(),
    SECRET_KEY: z.string().min(32),
  },
});
```

## Hot reload (development)

Watch `.env` files and reload when they change:

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  vars: { ... },
  watch: true,
  onReload: (changedFiles) => {
    console.log("Reloaded:", changedFiles);
  },
});
```

**Note**: `watch` is only available in Node.js and Bun. It is not available in the Edge build.

## Modes

Require specific variables in specific modes:

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  vars: {
    SENTRY_DSN: z.string().optional(),
    DATABASE_URL: z.string().url(),
  },
  modes: {
    production: ["SENTRY_DSN"],
  },
});
```

If `NODE_ENV=production` and `SENTRY_DSN` is not set, `createEnv` reports a validation error.

## Proxy methods

The returned env object also has proxy helpers for dynamic access:

```ts
env.get("PORT")               // typed get
env.get("MISSING", 3000)      // with fallback
env.has("PORT")               // boolean
env.raw("PORT")               // raw string before validation
env.toJSON()                  // plain object (sensitive keys redacted if configured)
```

## Redaction

Hide sensitive values in `toJSON()` output:

```ts
const env = createEnv({
  vars: { ... },
  redact: /SECRET|PASSWORD|KEY/i,
});

JSON.stringify(env.toJSON());
// sensitive keys are replaced with "[redacted]"
```

**Note**: Redaction only checks top-level keys. If you use `groups`, the key checked is the group name (e.g. `"db"`), not the nested property name (e.g. `"password"`). A pattern matching `"password"` will **not** redact `db.password` — only the entire `db` group would be redacted if `"db"` matched. To redact sensitive nested properties, either define them as top-level `vars` or match the group name instead.
